en.extract:
Grauer's swamp warbler (Bradypterus graueri) is a species of Old World warbler in the family Locustellidae. It is native to the Albertine Rift montane forests. Its natural habitats are freshwater lakes and freshwater marshes. It is threatened by habitat loss.
Grauer's swamp warbler is endemic to the Albertine Rift and is found in montane papyrus swamps above 1900m.  An investigation of the species population genetic structure revealed three clades across this region: clade 1, Virunga Volcanoes and Kigezi Highlands; clade 2, Rugege Highlands; and clade 3, Kahuzi-Biega Highlands (with clades 2 and 3 being sister groups).  The divergence between these clades is thought to be a result of landscape dynamics and a historic period of aridity.
The name commemorates the German zoologist Rudolf Grauer who collected natural history specimens in the Belgian Congo.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet. Archived 2009-01-03 at the Wayback Machine

'''Grauer's swamp warbler''' ('''''Bradypterus graueri''''') is a species of Old World warbler in the family [[Locustellidae]]. It is native to the [[Albertine Rift montane forests]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are freshwater [[lake]]s and freshwater [[marsh]]es. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

Grauer's swamp warbler is endemic to the Albertine Rift and is found in montane [[papyrus]] swamps above 1900m.  An investigation of the species population genetic structure revealed three clades across this region: clade 1, Virunga Volcanoes and Kigezi Highlands; clade 2, Rugege Highlands; and clade 3, Kahuzi-Biega Highlands (with clades 2 and 3 being sister groups).  The divergence between these clades is thought to be a result of landscape dynamics and a historic period of aridity.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Kahindo, C. M. |author2=Bates, J. M.  | author3=Bowie, R. C. K.  | year=2017| title=Population genetic structure of Grauer's Swamp Warbler ''Bradypterus graueri'', an Albertine Rift endemic| journal=Ibis| volume=159| pages=415–429 | doi=10.1111/ibi.12453 | issue=2| doi-access=free}}</ref>

The name commemorates the German zoologist [[Rudolf Grauer]] who collected natural history specimens in the Belgian Congo.<ref>{{cite book|last=Beolens|first=Bo|title=Whose Bird? Men and Women Commemorated in the Common Names of Birds|year=2003|publisher=Christopher Helm|location=London|page=94|author2=Watkins, Michael}}</ref>
